name: Bandit (Python Security Linter)
pricing_notes: Open source (Apache-2.0); PyCQA / Python Code Quality Authority

performance.notes: Analysis speed depends on codebase size

agent_readiness.notes: Static security analysis for Python. bandit -r . → scan recursively. bandit -r src/ -l -ii → low severity, medium confidence and above. bandit -r . -f json -o report.json → JSON output. bandit -r . --skip B101,B601 → skip specific checks. .bandit config file or pyproject.toml: [bandit] skips = B101,B601. Checks: B101 (assert usage), B108 (temp files), B201 (Flask debug), B301 (pickle), B501 (TLS), B601 (shell injection), B602 (subprocess shell=True), B608 (SQL injection), B701 (jinja2 autoescape). Severity: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH. Confidence: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH. Pre-commit hook integration. CI/CD ready. Plugins via stevedore. Ruff now includes some bandit rules (S prefix).

gotchas: High false positive rate for some checks (especially B101 asserts)
No dataflow analysis — misses complex injection paths
Some teams prefer semgrep for more customizable rules
Ruff now covers some bandit rules with better performance
Requires suppression comments (# nosec) which can mask real issues
best_when: Catching common Python security antipatterns (subprocess, pickle, SQL, TLS)
CI/CD security gate for Python projects
Quick security audit of Python codebases
avoid_when: When semgrep with custom rules provides better accuracy
When Ruff's S-prefix rules already cover the needed checks
Expecting SAST to replace security review (it finds patterns, not logic)
summary: Bandit is a Python security linter. bandit -r src/. Finds: subprocess shell injection, pickle, hardcoded passwords, SQL injection patterns, TLS issues, Flask debug mode. JSON/CSV/YAML output. CI-ready. Apache-2.0 by PyCQA. High false positive rate — tune with skip rules. Pre-commit integration. Ruff covers some bandit rules (S-prefix). Semgrep for more customizable rules. Essential baseline for Python security CI.
